county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hamilton County formed 1867 from unorganized territory. County seat is Aurora. Central Nebraska. Plainsman Museum. Burlington and Missouri River Railroad settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hamilton County (formed 1867, seat Aurora) records are at the courthouse in Aurora. The Plainsman Museum in Aurora holds regional genealogical and historical collections. Hamilton County was settled primarily by homesteaders in the late 1860s-1870s. BLM GLO records document original entries. The Burlington and Missouri River Railroad brought settlers and sold railroad land grants. German, Scandinavian, and Anglo-American families were the primary settlers. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. Hamilton County newspapers from the 1870s provide genealogical information. Aurora was also the site of an early public library movement in Nebraska.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Hamilton County. County Register of Deeds in Aurora holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Hamilton County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Hamilton County Register of Deeds, Aurora
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1867 to present. Nebraska is a public land state; initial federal patents are at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov). Subsequent transfers recorded at the county Register of Deeds. Includes deeds, mortgages, liens, and plats.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation

source_profiles.source_name: Hamilton County Marriage Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from 1867. Nebraska marriage licenses issued by county judges (County Court). State registration began 1909. Earlier marriages recorded at county level. FamilySearch has indexed many Nebraska marriages.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1867. Nebraska probate is handled by the County Court. Records include wills, letters of administration, estate inventories, guardianship files, and adoption records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Hamilton County (1870-1950). The 1890 census was destroyed by fire. Nebraska conducted a state census in 1885 (available on microfilm). county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Harlan County formed 1871 from unorganized territory. County seat is Alma. South-central Nebraska along the Republican River. Harlan County Reservoir (1952).
county_metadata.research_tips: Harlan County (formed 1871, seat Alma) in south-central Nebraska was carved from unorganized territory. Records are at the courthouse in Alma. The Republican River runs through the county; Harlan County Reservoir (1952) covered some former farmland. Homestead entries dominate early land records -- check BLM GLO records. Settlement came in the 1870s-1880s. German, Scandinavian, and Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The Harlan County Historical Society may have local materials. Republican City and Alma newspapers are useful local sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Harlan County. County Register of Deeds in Alma holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hayes County formed 1877 from unorganized territory. County seat is Hayes Center. Southwestern Nebraska. Sparsely populated. Ranching and farming.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hayes County (formed 1877, seat Hayes Center) in southwestern Nebraska was carved from unorganized territory. Records are at the courthouse in Hayes Center. This small, sparsely populated county was settled by homesteaders in the 1880s-1890s. BLM GLO records document homestead entries. The Stinking Water Creek area drew early ranchers. Anglo-American and German settlers predominated. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. The 1885 state census may have early enumeration. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. Researchers should check neighboring Frontier, Dundy, and Chase county records for related families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Hayes County. County Register of Deeds in Hayes Center holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hitchcock County formed 1873 from unorganized territory. County seat is Trenton. Southwestern Nebraska along the Republican River. Massacre Canyon battle site (1873, Sioux vs. Pawnee).
county_metadata.research_tips: Hitchcock County (formed 1873, seat Trenton) in southwestern Nebraska was carved from unorganized territory. Records are at the courthouse in Trenton. The Republican River valley runs through the county. Settlement came through homesteading in the 1870s-1880s. Culbertson and Trenton were the main communities. BLM GLO records document homestead entries. The Massacre Canyon battle site (1873) between Sioux and Pawnee is in Hitchcock County -- a significant event in Nebraska's Native American history. German, Scandinavian, and Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The Hitchcock County Historical Society may have local materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Hitchcock County. County Register of Deeds in Trenton holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Holt County formed 1860 from unorganized territory. County seat is O'Neill. Northeast Nebraska. Irish colonization movement (General John O'Neill). 'Irish Capital of Nebraska.' Parent to Boyd County (1891).
county_metadata.research_tips: Holt County (formed 1860, seat O'Neill) is one of northeast Nebraska's larger counties. Records are at the courthouse in O'Neill. O'Neill was founded by Irish settlers under General John O'Neill's Irish colonization movement in the 1870s -- Irish Catholic parish records are essential. The Holt County Historical Society has local materials. Holt County was parent to Boyd County (1891). Homestead entries at BLM GLO document early land claims. The Elkhorn River and Niobrara River drain the county. German and Scandinavian settlers also contributed to the population. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. O'Neill's designation as the 'Irish Capital of Nebraska' reflects the strong heritage -- Irish records connect to County Galway, County Mayo, and other western Irish origins.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Holt County. County Register of Deeds in O'Neill holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hooker County formed 1889 from unorganized territory. County seat is Mullen. Sandhills county. One of Nebraska's smallest populations. Cattle ranching. Middle Loup River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hooker County (formed 1889, seat Mullen) is a Sandhills county with one of Nebraska's smallest populations. Records are at the courthouse in Mullen. The Kinkaid Act (1904) drove permanent settlement with 640-acre homesteads. BLM GLO records are essential for land entries. Cattle ranching dominates the economy. The Middle Loup River runs through the county. The sparse population means very limited genealogical infrastructure. FamilySearch has minimal coverage. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. Federal censuses from 1900 onward are the primary population records. Researchers should check neighboring Thomas, Grant, and Cherry county records for related families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Hooker County. County Register of Deeds in Mullen holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Kinkaid Act Homestead Records -- Hooker County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kinkaid Act (1904) homestead records for Hooker County. The Kinkaid Act allowed 640-acre homestead claims in the Sandhills and other western Nebraska counties, recognizing that 160 acres was insufficient for ranching in semi-arid grasslands. Original case files at NARA; patents at BLM GLO.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Howard County formed 1871 from Hall County. County seat is St. Paul. Central Nebraska. Notable Danish settlement in the Dannebrog area. North and Middle Loup rivers.
county_metadata.research_tips: Howard County (formed 1871, seat St. Paul) records are at the courthouse in St. Paul. Howard County has notable Danish settlement -- the Dannebrog area was settled by Danish immigrants and remains a center of Danish-American heritage. Danish Lutheran church records are important sources. Homestead entries at BLM GLO document original settlers. The North Loup and Middle Loup rivers run through the county. Polish settlers also established communities. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The Howard County Historical Society may have local materials. Dannebrog's Danish heritage connects to Jutland and other Danish origins.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Howard County. County Register of Deeds in St. Paul holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jefferson County formed 1856 as original Nebraska Territory county. County seat is Fairbury. Rock Creek Station (Pony Express, Oregon Trail). Little Blue River valley.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County (formed 1856, seat Fairbury) records are at the courthouse in Fairbury. Jefferson County is one of Nebraska's earlier counties with territorial-era records. The Rock Creek Station historical site (Pony Express, Oregon Trail) is in Jefferson County. Homestead entries at BLM GLO document early settlers. The Little Blue River valley attracted settlement from the 1850s. German, Czech/Bohemian, and Anglo-American families predominated. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The territorial and 1885 state censuses are available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The Jefferson County Historical Society in Fairbury has local materials. Fairbury newspapers from the 1870s are valuable sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Jefferson County. County Register of Deeds in Fairbury holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Overland Trail Records -- Jefferson County Area
source_profiles.repository: History Nebraska / NARA / Oregon-California Trails Association
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Trail-era records for the Jefferson County area along the Oregon, California, and/or Mormon Trails. Emigrant diaries, station records, and military escort reports. History Nebraska holds manuscript collections. Most trail traffic passed through before county organization.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Johnson County formed 1855 from Nemaha County. County seat is Tecumseh. Southeastern Nebraska. Nemaha River. Territorial-era records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Johnson County (formed 1855, seat Tecumseh) records are at the courthouse in Tecumseh. Johnson County is one of the original southeastern Nebraska counties with territorial-era records. Homestead and pre-emption entries at BLM GLO document early settlers. The Nemaha River runs through the county. German, Anglo-American, and Irish settlers predominated. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The territorial and 1885 state censuses are available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The Johnson County Historical Society may have local materials. Tecumseh's early newspapers provide genealogical information. Johnson County was carved from Nemaha County -- earlier records may be in Nemaha County files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Johnson County. County Register of Deeds in Tecumseh holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Johnson County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Johnson County Register of Deeds, Tecumseh
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1855 to present. Nebraska is a public land state; initial federal patents are at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov). Subsequent transfers recorded at the county Register of Deeds. Includes deeds, mortgages, liens, and plats.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation

source_profiles.source_name: Johnson County Marriage Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from 1855. Nebraska marriage licenses issued by county judges (County Court). State registration began 1909. Earlier marriages recorded at county level. FamilySearch has indexed many Nebraska marriages.

source_profiles.source_name: Johnson County Probate and Estate Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1855. Nebraska probate is handled by the County Court. Records include wills, letters of administration, estate inventories, guardianship files, and adoption records.

source_profiles.source_name: Johnson County Federal and State Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Johnson County (1860-1950). The 1890 census was destroyed by fire. Nebraska conducted a state census in 1885 (available on microfilm). Territorial censuses (1854-1856, 1860) exist for early counties. Available through FamilySearch and Ancestry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: 1890 federal census destroyed nationally; Nebraska 1885 state census survives

source_profiles.source_name: Johnson County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Johnson County including homestead entries, pre-emption claims, timber culture entries, cash sales, and military warrant locations. Nebraska is a public land state -- all original land transfers from federal ownership are documented here. Searchable online by name and location.

source_profiles.source_name: Johnson County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / Historical Society
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Johnson County Historical Society / Nebraska State Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions and burial records for Johnson County. Find A Grave has growing coverage. Township, church, and family cemeteries are common in rural Nebraska. County historical societies and the Nebraska State Genealogical Society have published cemetery transcription volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Johnson County Records on Microfilm -- History Nebraska
source_profiles.coverage_notes: History Nebraska (NSHS) holds microfilm copies of Johnson County records including deed indexes, probate records, marriage records, county board proceedings, and selected court records. Also holds manuscript collections, photographs, and maps related to Johnson County history.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Kearney County formed 1860 from unorganized territory. County seat is Minden. South-central Nebraska. Distinct from the city of Kearney (Buffalo County). Harold Warp Pioneer Village.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kearney County (formed 1860, seat Minden) in south-central Nebraska was carved from unorganized territory. Records are at the courthouse in Minden. The Harold Warp Pioneer Village in Minden is a notable regional attraction. Homestead entries at BLM GLO document early settlers. The Platte River borders the county to the north. German, Scandinavian, and Anglo-American settlers predominated. Note: Kearney County is distinct from the city of Kearney in Buffalo County. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The Kearney County Historical Society may have local materials. Minden newspapers from the 1870s-1880s are useful sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Kearney County. County Register of Deeds in Minden holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1860 to present. Nebraska is a public land state; initial federal patents are at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov). Subsequent transfers recorded at the county Register of Deeds. Includes deeds, mortgages, liens, and plats.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation
